Next up: Post 11 — Writing Your Family AI Agreement: A Step-by-Step Guide

Notes

  1. Bender, Emily M., Timnit Gebru, Angelina McMillan-Major, and Shmargaret Shmitchell. "On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?" FAccT '21: Proceedings of the 2021 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, March 2021. https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3442188.3445922 ↩

  2. MediaSmarts. "Resources for Parents — AI and Algorithms." MediaSmarts, 2024. https://mediasmarts.ca/digital-media-literacy/general-information/ai-and-algorithms/resources-parents-ai-and-algorithms ↩

entry.title: Part 9 - Deepfakes Are Real: What Parents Need to See to Understand
entry.summary: Deepfakes — AI-generated fake images, video, and audio — are already being used to harm people. Here's what parents need to understand, and what to do if something goes wrong.
entry.content:
Part 9 of 12 in the Try It Yourself series — practical AI guidance for parents at blog.parentsguidetoai.ca

This post is a bit different from the others. It covers something serious.

We've spent the previous posts on practical skills and hands-on exercises. This one is about a harm that is already happening to young people — including children in BC — and that parents need to understand before it affects their family.

The parent's guide covers this in Section 12. We're covering it here because it deserves more than a brief mention.

What deepfakes actually are

The word "deepfake" comes from "deep learning" (a type of AI) and "fake." It refers to AI-generated media — images, video, or audio — that depicts real people doing or saying things they never actually did.

The technology has been around for several years, but it has gotten dramatically more accessible. Tools that once required significant technical skill and computing power now run in apps and websites that anyone can access.

The result: a realistic-looking fake image of a real person can now be created in minutes, by someone with no technical background, using only a photograph.

Why deepfakes are harder to spot than you expect

Many parents assume that fake images and videos look obviously fake. This is less and less true.

There is no reliable visual checklist — the "look for six fingers" advice has not kept up with the current generation of tools. Newer deepfakes often pass casual inspection, particularly in still images. Video deepfakes are still sometimes detectable, but audio deepfakes (voice cloning) are frequently indistinguishable from real recordings.

The parent's guide is direct about this:

"Even with scrutiny, these fakes are often extremely hard to detect."

This is not meant to be alarming — it's meant to set accurate expectations. When we tell children "you can just tell," we're not being honest.

The specific harms targeting young people

Two types of harm appear repeatedly in the research, in news coverage, and in the resources compiled by organizations like MediaSmarts and the Canadian Centre for Child Protection.

Non-consensual intimate images (NCII): AI tools are being used to generate fake explicit images of real people — including minors — from ordinary photographs. These images are then distributed online or used for harassment. The person depicted didn't consent, wasn't present, and may not know the image exists until it has already spread.

Sextortion: This refers to coercive threats — "pay money or I'll share these images" — using real or fabricated intimate images. Sextortion cases targeting teenagers have increased significantly, including cases where the images used are AI-generated fakes.1 The target can be victimized even if the images are not real.

Both of these are crimes in Canada. British Columbia has specific legislation covering this.

BC legal protections

In 2023, British Columbia passed the Intimate Images Protection Act. The law applies to both real and AI-generated intimate images distributed without consent. It provides legal remedies including the ability to seek a tribunal or court order requiring platforms to remove content.2

The parent's guide summarizes this:

"British Columbia has legal protections that cover these situations."

This doesn't mean the process is easy or fast. It means there is a legal framework, and it is worth knowing it exists.

What to do if this happens

If your child is affected — whether through NCII, sextortion, or any related situation — the following resources are specifically designed to help:

Take Back Your Images (BC Intimate Images Protection Service): A free service that creates a digital fingerprint of an intimate image so it can be detected and removed from participating platforms. This tool can be used proactively, before anything happens, and it can also be used after.

NeedHelpNow.ca: Designed specifically for young people in Canada. If an intimate image of a minor is circulating online, this site provides step-by-step guidance on reporting and removal. It is specifically designed for teens, and the language reflects that.

Cybertip.ca: The Canadian Centre for Child Protection's online reporting line for child sexual exploitation material. If AI-generated intimate images of a minor are involved, this is the appropriate reporting channel in Canada.

ERASE (Expect Respect and A Safe Education): BC's school-based safe schools reporting system. Schools can be a resource, and ERASE provides a way to involve the school if the situation started there or involves classmates.

Kids Help Phone: 1-800-668-6868 (24/7), or text CONNECT to 686868. For young people who need to talk to someone right now, Kids Help Phone has counsellors available around the clock.

None of these resources require you to know in advance whether the situation is "serious enough." They are designed for exactly the kind of uncertainty most families face in these moments. Call, report, or reach out — the organizations will help you figure out next steps.

Having the conversation before something goes wrong

The parent's guide recommends talking about this before a crisis. That advice is correct and worth repeating.

Waiting until your child is already in a difficult situation to explain deepfakes, consent, and what to do is harder for both of you. A low-stakes conversation now — "have you heard of deepfakes? here's what they are and here's what to do if something ever worries you" — gives your child a framework before they need it.

Some families find it easier to start this conversation by reading a news article together. Others find it easier to connect it to the parent's guide: "I've been reading this guide, and there's a section I think we should talk about."

However you get there: the conversation is worth having.

The short version:

  • Deepfakes are AI-generated fake images, video, and audio. They are increasingly realistic and easy to create.
  • The most serious harms involve non-consensual intimate images (NCII) and sextortion targeting young people.
  • BC's Intimate Images Protection Act covers both real and AI-generated intimate images.
  • If your family is affected: Take Back Your Images, NeedHelpNow.ca, Cybertip.ca, ERASE, Kids Help Phone (1-800-668-6868).
  • Have the conversation now, not after something goes wrong.

The next post returns to a practical skill that complements everything in this series: how to recognize when AI answers reflect bias — and how to teach that skill to your children.

Read Section 12 of the parent's guide: parentsguidetoai.ca — it covers this topic in full, with additional links and resources. Bookmark the resources above regardless of whether you need them right now.

Next up: Post 10 — Recognizing Bias in AI Answers: A Skill Every Parent Can Learn

Notes

  1. Canadian Centre for Child Protection. "Statistics — Sextortion." Protect Children, 2024. https://www.protectchildren.ca/en/resources-research/statistics/ ↩

  2. Province of British Columbia. "Intimate Images Protection Act." Province of British Columbia, 2024. https://www2.gov.bc.ca/gov/content/safety/public-safety/intimate-images/intimate-images-consent ↩

entry.title: Part 8 - AI for Busy, Overwhelmed, or Differently-Abled Parents
entry.summary: AI's accessibility features aren't just for students. Here are four concrete ways AI can help parents with translation, summarization, planning, and drafting.
entry.content:
Part 8 of 12 in the Try It Yourself series — practical AI guidance for parents at blog.parentsguidetoai.ca

The parent's guide at parentsguidetoai.ca has a section on how AI can help students who face challenges with reading, writing, language barriers, organization, or executive functioning. It's one of the most practical parts of the guide.

What it doesn't say explicitly — but what's worth saying here — is that every one of those challenges also applies to parents.

Parenting is time-compressed. School communications arrive in dense documents at inconvenient moments. Many parents are navigating systems in a language that isn't their first. Many live with ADHD, dyslexia, or other conditions that make reading long text or managing competing priorities genuinely hard.

AI doesn't solve all of that. But it helps more than most people realize. This post covers four concrete applications for parents.

Translation and language support

If English is not your primary language, communicating with schools can be frustrating. Official documents are often written in formal, institutional language — the kind that's hard even for native speakers.

Google Gemini and ChatGPT both handle translation well.1 You can paste a document in English and ask for a translation into another language. You can also ask for the explanation of a concept in your preferred language, and then read about it in the language that makes the most sense to you.

Try a prompt like:

"Please translate this school newsletter into [your language], using plain everyday language."

Then paste the document text.

I'll use a personal example here, I grew up in a bilingual (English primary, and French) household, and took French immersion classes as a child, but I haven't regularly written or spoken French in many years. My children are in French immersion, and while my French has come back over the years, I still make use of AI translation tools when there are assignments that use scientific language that doesn't easily translate, or when there are complex word problems in math or other assignments.

You can also use AI in the other direction — drafting a message to a teacher in English when that's not your first language. Type your thoughts in your own language, then ask AI to help you write a clear email in English. Read it carefully before you send it to make sure it says what you intended.

Summarization for the time-crunched parent

Most school communications are longer than they need to be. Policy updates, newsletter updates, field trip information packages, acceptable use agreements — the average school parent reads a significant amount of administrative text every year.

AI summarization is genuinely useful here. Paste any document into ChatGPT, Copilot, or Claude and ask:

"Summarize this in five bullet points. Include any action items that require my response."

The response will be faster and shorter than reading the full document. Check it against the original for anything important — AI summaries occasionally drop key details. But for a first pass on a long document, it's a real time-saver.

One particularly useful application: the parent's guide itself. The full guide is thorough and well-organized, but it's also long. If there's a section you want to understand quickly — the deepfakes section, the privacy section, the section on Copilot 13+ — paste it into an AI tool and ask for a plain-language summary. Then go back to the original for anything that needs more detail.

Planning, organizing, and drafting

For parents who find organization challenging — including those managing ADHD or other executive functioning difficulties — AI can help with the parts of parenting admin that involve getting information out of your head and into an organized form.

Some practical examples:

Drafting emails to teachers or schools. Tell AI what you want to communicate, and ask it to help you write a clear, professional email. You provide the substance; AI helps with the structure and wording.

Creating a weekly schedule. Describe your child's activities, school times, and other commitments, and ask AI to help you organize them into a weekly plan.

Preparing for parent-teacher meetings. As we covered in Post 6, AI is useful for generating questions ahead of a meeting. It's also useful for organizing the notes you take during one.

Breaking down a complex task. If you're trying to figure out how to handle a situation at school — a conflict, a concern about your child's learning, a question about policy — you can talk through the situation with AI and ask it to help you identify next steps.

One way that I use AI is to help manage and coordinate schedules. I use AI tools like Gemini that is built into my Google account to find emails for kids sports and events and make sure they are all added to my calendar, and scan emails related to them to make sure I have followed up on any requests or actions. This is especially important for teams and activities where my wife or I are organizers or have obligations (such as being safety or score keeper for my daughters hockey team).

AI writing tools built into things you already use

You may already have AI assistance available in tools you use every day without realizing it.

If you use Google Docs, Gemini AI is integrated and can help you draft, rewrite, or summarize directly in the document. Look for the Gemini icon in the toolbar.2

If you use Microsoft Word or Outlook, Copilot is available in many versions and can help with writing and summarization. Look for the Copilot icon in the app.3

These are worth knowing about because they don't require you to open a separate tool. The assistance is available where you're already working.

As always: don't paste private information about your child or other people into these tools, and review everything AI helps you write before you send or publish it.

The short version:

  • AI's accessibility features aren't just for students — they're useful for parents too.
  • Translation: paste school documents and ask for a summary in your preferred language.
  • Summarization: get the key points from long school communications faster.
  • Planning and drafting: AI can help structure your thoughts into emails, schedules, and meeting prep.
  • Google Docs and Microsoft Word have AI features built in — you may already have access.

We've covered a lot of practical ground. The next post shifts tone a bit. We're going to look at one of the more serious risks the parent's guide covers: deepfakes. Understanding this isn't optional for parents right now.

Try it today: Paste the last long document you received from your child's school into ChatGPT or Copilot and ask for a five-bullet summary. Note what it captures well and what it misses.

Next up: Post 9 — Deepfakes Are Real: What Parents Need to See to Understand

Notes

  1. Google. "Bringing State-of-the-Art Gemini Translation Capabilities to Google Translate." Google Blog, December 2025. https://blog.google/products-and-platforms/products/search/gemini-capabilities-translation-upgrades/ ↩

  2. Google. "New Gemini Capabilities in Google Docs Help You Go from Blank Page to Brilliance." Google Workspace Updates Blog, April 2026. https://workspaceupdates.googleblog.com/2026/04/new-gemini-capabilities-in-google-docs-help-you-go-from-blank-page-to-brilliance.html ↩

  3. Microsoft. "AI Email Writing with Copilot in Outlook." Microsoft 365, 2026. https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365-life-hacks/everyday-ai/communication-skills/ai-email-writing-with-copilot-in-outlook ↩

entry.title: Part 7 - School AI vs. Home AI: What Every VSB Parent Should Understand
entry.summary: The school version of Copilot and the home version share a name but operate under very different rules. Here's what VSB parents need to know.
entry.content:
Part 7 of 12 in the Try It Yourself series — practical AI guidance for parents at blog.parentsguidetoai.ca

In May 2026, the Vancouver School Board announced that eligible secondary students would have access to Microsoft Copilot 13+ through school accounts.1 If your child is a VSB secondary student, they may already be using it — or will be soon.

Here's the thing: Copilot 13+ and the Copilot you can download on your phone are not the same product. They share the same name and the same underlying technology, but they operate under very different rules.

This post explains those differences — so you know what protections exist at school, what gaps exist at home, and what questions are worth asking.

It's also important to make a disclaimer here - my understanding of the differences is based on publicly available documentation from Microsoft, and from the VSB announcements. I don't have an inside track on the actual terms and conditions, or what agreements that VSB and Microsoft have in place besides what has been published. If you learn about or find mistakes in this page, I would appreciate you reporting them to me by opening an issue here!

What Copilot 13+ includes that the public version doesn't

Section 2 of the parent's guide lists the protections that the VSB's school version of Copilot may include. This is worth reading carefully, because it explains why the school made the choice it made — and why "it's just Copilot" isn't a complete picture.

According to the VSB, the school version includes:

  • School-managed accounts — created and controlled by the school district, not by your child personally
  • Commercial data protection — conversations may not be used to train public AI models
  • Content filtering — designed to reduce exposure to harmful material
  • Encrypted data handling
  • No use of student prompts to train public AI models
  • Integration with school digital citizenship expectations2

Compare that to the public version of Copilot, which you might access at copilot.microsoft.com with a personal Microsoft account. The public version is useful — we recommended it in Post 1 as an easy starting point. But it doesn't come with those same protections by default.

This matters because your child may not distinguish between "the AI I use at school" and "the AI I can use at home." From their perspective, it looks like the same thing. But from a data protection standpoint, it isn't.

Why "school-approved" doesn't automatically mean "home-approved"

The parent's guide offers a principle that's simple and worth repeating at home:

"School-approved does not automatically mean home-approved." — A Parent's Guide to Artificial Intelligence

This isn't about being more restrictive than the school. It's about understanding that the school's approval covers specific tools, in specific ways, with specific protections in place. Those conditions don't automatically carry over to a personal account at home. The school decisions don't necessarily reflect your values, or your families values. Your concerns about the safety, integrity, environmental impact, origin of AI training materials, or any other opinions you have are what should inform the decisions you make for you and your family.

A useful analogy: a school may approve field trips in school vehicles driven by certified drivers. That doesn't mean you'd let your child ride in any vehicle with anyone. The underlying activity (riding in a vehicle) is the same. The safety context is different.

We're not suggesting that home AI use is dangerous. Plenty of the tools in this series can be appropriate for family use, they just might not be right for your family. The decision about what's appropriate at home is yours to make — and making it deliberately is better than letting it happen by default.

What the ongoing debate looks like from a VSB parent's perspective

When the VSB announced Copilot 13+, not everyone was immediately in favour. The parent's guide notes that some students and parents raised concerns publicly, including:

  • Privacy concerns
  • Impact on independent thinking and learning
  • Environmental costs
  • Student safety

There was also a request for clearer policies and the option to opt out.3

These are reasonable concerns. If you share them, you're in good company — and you have the right to ask questions. The guide puts it clearly:

"If you have concerns, you are not alone, and you are entitled to raise them."

Families who want to limit or opt out of school-provided AI tools can contact the school or district directly. Asking is always the right move when you're unsure.

Questions worth asking your child's school

If you haven't already asked about AI at your child's school, here are some useful starting points:

  • Which AI tool is being used, and is it a school-managed account?
  • What data protections apply? Is there a privacy policy or acceptable use policy I can read?
  • Can my child opt out, and what does that look like in practice?
  • Are teachers trained to support students in using AI appropriately?
  • How is AI use explained to students in class?

You can find the VSB's current guidance at the VSB Digital Literacy and Use of AI in Education page and the VSB Copilot 13+ page. The parent's guide links to both of these in Appendix B.

It's also worth noting that the opinions on AI use may be very dynamic in your home. I am an AI skeptic, despite using and working with it every day. One of my children doesn't like AI technology at all and has strong opinions about it's use, even how I use it at work! My other child doesn't feel terribly strongly about it, and sees it as a useful tool for somethings, and a neat toy for others. These are important discussions to have with your children to help inform them of why you might make certain parenting choices, and to understand the impacts it has on you, your children, and their learning at school.

The short version:

  • Copilot 13+ (the school version) has data protections that the public home version doesn't automatically include.
  • "School-approved" doesn't automatically mean "home-approved" — the context is different.
  • Parents have the right to ask questions and request opt-out options.
  • Check the VSB's official guidance pages for current information, since policies are still evolving.
  • The questions to ask: What tool, what account type, what data protections, can we opt out?

The next post takes a different angle — not what AI is used for, but who it can help. We'll look at how AI's accessibility features can support parents who face challenges with reading, writing, language, or organization.

Check the VSB guidance: Visit the VSB Digital Literacy and Use of AI in Education page and bookmark it. Also bookmark Appendix B of the parent's guide — it's a maintained list of official links.

Next up: Post 8 — AI for Busy, Overwhelmed, or Differently-Abled Parents

Notes

  1. "AI Chatbots Are Coming to BC Classrooms." The Tyee, May 28, 2026. https://thetyee.ca/News/2026/05/28/AI-Chatbots-Coming-BC-Classrooms/ ↩

  2. Vancouver School Board. "Copilot 13+ in Secondary Schools." Vancouver School Board, 2026. https://www.vsb.bc.ca/copilot-13-in-secondary-schools; Microsoft Support. "Data Protection When Using Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat for Work or School." Microsoft, 2026. https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/privacy/data-protection-when-using-microsoft-365-copilot-chat-for-work-or-school ↩

  3. "AI Chatbots Are Coming to BC Classrooms." The Tyee, May 28, 2026. https://thetyee.ca/News/2026/05/28/AI-Chatbots-Coming-BC-Classrooms/ ↩

entry.title: Part 6 - Using AI as Your Personal Research Assistant
entry.summary: AI is genuinely useful for learning tasks: summarizing documents, explaining concepts, and generating questions. Here are three concrete things to try this week.
entry.content:
Part 6 of 12 in the Try It Yourself series — practical AI guidance for parents at blog.parentsguidetoai.ca

We've spent a few posts looking at what AI gets wrong. This one is about what it gets right.

When AI is used as a learning tool — to understand something, to get a summary, to break a complex idea into plain language — it's genuinely useful. And these are skills that matter as much for parents as they do for children.

Section 6 of the parent's guide describes AI's learning support uses with students in mind. But every example in that list also works for a parent who is trying to understand something quickly. This post gives you three concrete tasks to try this week, with instructions.

Why learning support isn't just for students

The parent's guide puts it plainly: AI can help by explaining difficult concepts, summarizing complex reading, creating practice quizzes, and generating study guides.

Think about how often a version of that would be useful for you.

You get a three-page letter from your child's school about a new policy. You need to understand it, but you're already late for something else. A twenty-second AI summary would save you ten minutes of slow reading.

Your child asks for help with a topic you barely remember from school — or never learned at all. AI can give you a quick refresher in plain language before you sit down together.

You want to understand AI well enough to have a real conversation with your child's teacher. You could read several articles, or you could ask AI to explain the key ideas and then use Post 5's verification routine to check what matters.

One example of how I use these tools every day, is to kick off a security analysis. I have to start by building an understanding of the business, system, or application that I am going to analyze, so using an AI tool to quickly assess all of documents, source code, or information that I have to explain where everything is, and organize the assessment I need to do is a great way to quickly estimate the size and complexity of the task ahead of me. I still need to build the knowledge and do the analysis, but sizing the project quickly is very helpful in prioritizing my work and organizing my tasks.

This is the difference between using AI to understand something and using AI to produce something. When you use AI to understand, you still do the thinking. The AI is a translator, not a ghostwriter.

Three things to try this week

1. Summarize a school document

Find the last long letter, policy document, or newsletter you received from your child's school. Open ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, or Claude and type something like:

"Here is a document from my child's school. Please summarize it in plain language, in five bullet points."

Then paste the document text. The AI will give you a clear summary in seconds. Check it against the original to make sure nothing important got dropped.

This is a low-stakes way to practice using AI for something useful, and to get a feel for how much you can trust the summary on different types of content.

One thing to watch: Don't paste anything in the document that includes personal student information — names, ID numbers, addresses. If the document is generic (a school newsletter, a policy overview), it's fine. If it includes your child's specific personal details, remove those first.

2. Ask AI to explain a term from the parent's guide glossary

The parent's guide at parentsguidetoai.ca includes a glossary of AI and digital literacy terms — from "algorithm" to "training data." Pick any term you weren't completely sure about and ask AI to explain it.

Try a prompt like:

"Explain what a 'large language model' is in plain language, as if you're talking to a parent who doesn't have a tech background."

Then read the response. Does it match what you understood from the guide? Does it add anything useful? Is it actually in plain language, or did it still sneak in some jargon?

This exercise does two things. It builds your understanding of AI terminology. And it gives you a feel for how AI handles explanation tasks — which is directly relevant to how your child might use it for learning.

3. Generate questions for a parent-teacher conversation about AI

This one is a bit more creative. If you have a parent-teacher conference coming up — or just want to be prepared for one — try this prompt:

"I'm a parent of a secondary school student in Vancouver, BC. My child's school is rolling out Microsoft Copilot for students. What questions should I ask the teacher about how AI is being used in the classroom? I don't know much about AI or the risks, Ask me some questions to help me understand better what I might be concerned about."

The list you get will probably include some obvious questions and some you hadn't thought of. That's useful. It's not a replacement for your own thinking, but it's a good starting point.

The distinction that matters: understanding vs. producing

This is worth saying clearly, because it applies to both parents and children.

Using AI to understand means: you read the AI's response, you process it, and then you think and act on your own. The output of that process is your thinking, informed by the AI.

Using AI to produce means: you take the AI's output and present it as your own, without really engaging with it. The output of that process is the AI's thinking, with your name on it.

The parent's guide applies this distinction to students and homework. But it applies to adult life too. If you ask AI to draft an email to your child's teacher and send it without reading it carefully, you're presenting AI output as your own without checking it. That's a different kind of risk — not academic integrity, but accuracy and tone.

AI is a starting point. Your judgment is the final step.

The short version:

  • AI is genuinely useful for learning tasks: summarizing, explaining, generating questions.
  • The key distinction: using AI to understand something (good) vs. using it to produce something you didn't think through (risky).
  • Three tasks to try: summarize a school document, ask AI to explain a glossary term, generate parent-teacher questions.
  • For these tasks, Claude and Perplexity are particularly good choices.
  • Check the output — AI summaries can miss things, and AI explanations can still include jargon.

We've been focused on your own AI use. The next post zooms back out to the school context — and explains what's different about the AI your VSB student is using at school versus the tools you've been trying at home.

Try it today: Use AI to explain one term from the parent's guide glossary that you weren't fully sure about. Read the response, then read the guide's definition. Notice where they agree and where they differ.

Next up: Post 7 — School AI vs. Home AI: What Every VSB Parent Should Understand

entry.title: Part 5 - How to Spot AI Mistakes Before Your Kids Don't
entry.summary: AI mistakes look identical to correct answers. This post gives you a simple routine for catching errors — one you can use yourself and teach to your children.
entry.content:
Part 5 of 12 in the Try It Yourself series — practical AI guidance for parents at blog.parentsguidetoai.ca

AI makes mistakes that look exactly like correct answers. That's what makes them worth paying attention to.

We covered this briefly in Post 2 — AI produces confident-sounding output regardless of whether it's accurate. In this post, we get practical. You're going to learn a simple routine for catching AI errors, so you can use it yourself and teach it to your children.

The good news: this doesn't require any special skills. It mostly requires slowing down for sixty seconds before you trust what you're reading.

The four questions the parent's guide gives students

Section 7 of the parent's guide lists four questions students should ask about AI-generated information. They're worth reading as a parent too, because they apply just as well to adults:

  • Where did this information come from?
  • Can I verify it?
  • Does another source agree?
  • Could this be fabricated?

These are good questions. The challenge is that they assume you know how to follow up on the answers. So let's make that concrete.

Where AI most often goes wrong

Not all AI errors are equally likely. Once you know the patterns, you'll catch mistakes faster.

Citations and academic sources are the highest-risk category. AI will generate plausible-looking citations, author names, journal titles, article titles, volume numbers, that simply do not exist. The citation looks real. The paper doesn't. This happens with regularity, even with the best AI tools, and it doesn't just happen in education - it's happened with scientific journals,1 published reports from companies known for doing excellent consulting work, and many times in legal proceedings.2

If your child's homework references an academic paper or study, verify it exists before it gets submitted. You can also use a simple prompt to help you with this, something like "Review the attached document, and it's references, and verify that the references and citations actually exist, and prepare a step by step guide on how to validate them myself."

Statistics and specific numbers are another common problem. AI will state figures — percentages, populations, dollar amounts, growth rates — with the same confidence whether they're accurate or invented. Numbers feel authoritative. They're not always right.

Names, dates, and local information are also risky. Details about real people (including minor public figures), specific events, dates of legislation, or information specific to a region — like BC policy or Vancouver schools — are more likely to be wrong than general information.

Recent events are a particular vulnerability. AI systems have a knowledge cutoff — they were trained on data up to a certain date, and they don't reliably know what happened after that.3 The parent's guide itself notes this:

"AI tools, school policies, and support laws change quickly."

If you're asking AI about something from the last year or two, verify it independently.

A practical verification routine

Here's what to actually do when you want to check something AI told you.

Step 1: Identify the specific claim. Don't try to verify the whole response. Pick the most important fact — the one the rest of the response depends on. That's what to check.

Step 2: Use Perplexity for the same question. Perplexity is designed to show its sources inline. Ask it the same question and look at what sources it cites. If the sources are real and say what Perplexity says they say, that's a good sign. If Perplexity can't find sources, that's also useful information.

Step 3: Go to one primary source. Don't just check other AI tools — they may have the same wrong information. Go to an official site, a known news organization, or a government resource. For BC-specific information, the BC Ministry of Education and Vancouver School Board sites are reliable.

Step 4: For academic citations, search the title. If AI cited a paper, search for the exact title in Google Scholar or through a library database. If you can't find it, the paper probably doesn't exist.

This routine takes about ninety seconds for a straightforward claim. It takes longer for complex research. But it's the difference between using AI as a starting point and using it as the final word.

Teaching this to your kids

Here's the thing about verification: if you've done this yourself, you can explain it to your child in terms of a real example. That's much more effective than a general warning.

The parent's guide suggests asking: "Could you explain this work without using the AI?" A companion question that's equally useful: "Did you check whether this is actually true?"

Neither of these questions is an accusation. They're the same questions a good teacher or editor asks about any piece of work. AI just makes them more necessary.

The short version:

  • AI mistakes look identical to correct information — there's no warning label.
  • The riskiest areas: citations (often invented), statistics (often fabricated), local/recent information (often outdated or wrong).
  • A simple routine: identify the key claim, check it in Perplexity for sourced results, then verify at a primary source.
  • Doing this yourself gives you a real example to share with your child.
  • The two questions to ask: "Could you explain this without the AI?" and "Did you check whether it's true?"

Now that you can spot mistakes, we're ready to look at what AI is genuinely good for. The next post is about using AI as your own personal research assistant — and how to do it without the same pitfalls we've been discussing.

Try it today: Take a piece of AI-assisted content — something your child brought home, or something you generated yourself this week — and run one claim through the verification routine. See if it holds up.

Next up: Post 6 — Using AI as Your Personal Research Assistant

Notes

  1. GPTZero. "GPTZero Finds 100 New Hallucinations in NeurIPS 2025 Accepted Papers." GPTZero, 2026. https://gptzero.me/news/neurips/ ↩

  2. Wikipedia contributors. "Mata v. Avianca, Inc." Wikipedia, 2023–2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mata_v._Avianca,_Inc. ↩

  3. Otterly.ai. "LLM Knowledge Cutoff Dates: Every Major AI Model's Training Data Cutoff." Otterly.ai, 2026. https://otterly.ai/blog/knowledge-cutoff/ ↩

entry.title: Part 4 - Your Privacy Checklist: What Never to Share With AI
entry.summary: Before you spend much time with AI tools, there's one thing you need to understand: what you type doesn't always stay private. Here's what to know and what to check.
entry.content:
Part 4 of 12 in the Try It Yourself series — practical AI guidance for parents at blog.parentsguidetoai.ca

Before you spend much time with AI tools, there's one thing you need to understand. What you type into an AI tool doesn't always stay private. The prompts you enter are logged, and held for a period time that could be different for each AI tool.

This isn't a reason to avoid AI, after all most internet tools do the same thing. It's a reason to use it thoughtfully — the same way you'd think twice before posting something publicly online. The parent's guide at parentsguidetoai.ca gives children a clear rule about what to keep private. That rule applies equally to parents who are trying these tools for the first time.

This post covers what not to share, why it matters, and how to check the privacy settings on the tools you're using.

The rule from the parent's guide — and why it applies to you too

Section 7 of the parent's guide includes a privacy checklist for children. It lists things that should never be entered into an AI tool:

  • Passwords
  • Home addresses
  • Financial information
  • Health information
  • Student identification numbers
  • Personal information about classmates
  • Family information
  • Private photographs

And it offers a principle worth remembering:

"Never put anything into an AI tool that you would not want copied, stored, or shared." — A Parent's Guide to Artificial Intelligence

This applies to adults just as much as children. If you're trying an AI tool for the first time this week, it's easy to start typing things into it the same way you'd type into a search engine. But a search engine query and an AI conversation are not the same thing.

When you search something on Google, Google sees the query. When you type something into ChatGPT or Copilot, that whole conversation — every question, every response — may be retained by the company that runs the tool.

It's kind of a silly example, but when I was taking a writing class, one of the assignments was to write an action sequence, and I decided to write one about two police officers working to defuse a bomb while a gang of villains tried to stop them. Naturally I wanted to look up how to write a somewhat realistic bomb would look and behave like. ChatGPT was not very enthusiastic about helping me figure that out...

How AI tools use your conversations

Different tools handle data differently. But as a general rule, conversations with public AI tools may be:

  • Stored by the company for a period of time
  • Reviewed by human staff for safety and quality purposes (this is more common than people realize)
  • Used to train future AI models — unless you opt out1

That last point is the one that surprises most people. When you try a free AI tool with the default settings turned on, your conversations may become part of the data that makes future versions of the tool smarter. You're not just a user. In some configurations, you're also a contributor.

The good news is that most major tools give you the ability to turn this off. And the managed school versions, like VSB's Copilot 13+, include additional protections specifically because students are minors. The parent's guide covers this in Section 2:

"Student prompts and conversations are not being used to train public AI models"2

That protection applies to the school tool. The home version may have different defaults.

Finding the privacy settings on the three main tools

Here's where to look on each tool you might be using:

ChatGPT (chatgpt.com): - Log in and click your profile icon (bottom left) - Go to Settings → Data Controls - Look for "Improve the model for everyone" — turn this off if you don't want your conversations used for training - You can also delete individual conversations or your entire conversation history from this menu

Microsoft Copilot (copilot.microsoft.com): - If you're signed in with a Microsoft account, your data is governed by Microsoft's privacy policy - For the school version (Copilot 13+), data protections are set by the school district — not your personal settings - Check your Microsoft account privacy settings at account.microsoft.com/privacy

Google Gemini (gemini.google.com): - Log in and click the three-dot menu or your profile icon - Go to Gemini Apps Activity to see and delete stored conversations - You can turn off activity saving, which stops conversations from being stored in your account history

It takes about two minutes to check each of these. I'd suggest doing it the first time you open the tool.

A practical approach

The privacy settings matter. But the more reliable protection is habit.

Think of AI conversations as semi-public. Not the same as posting on social media, but not as private as a conversation you'd have with your doctor or your bank. With that framing, most everyday AI use is fine, asking questions, getting explanations, working through an idea. The things you'd want to keep private mostly take care of themselves.

For children, this is worth making explicit. The parent's guide suggests treating AI conversations like public conversations: a simple, clear standard that's easy for a child to understand and apply.

The short version:

  • AI conversations may be stored, reviewed by humans, and used to train future models — check the settings.
  • The rule from the parent's guide applies to everyone: never type anything you wouldn't want copied, stored, or shared.
  • School-managed tools (like VSB's Copilot 13+) have stronger data protections than the home versions.
  • On ChatGPT, Copilot, and Gemini, you can turn off data sharing — it takes about two minutes.
  • The most practical protection is treating AI like a semi-public space.

With privacy covered, we're ready to move into the hands-on part of the series. The next post is about one of the most important skills any AI user can develop: how to spot mistakes in what AI tells you before your kids don't.

Check your settings: Open whichever AI tool you signed up for and find the privacy or data controls. Turn off conversation training if that option exists. Then review Section 7 of the parent's guide with your child.

Next up: Post 5 — How to Spot AI Mistakes Before Your Kids Don't

Notes

  1. OpenAI. "How Your Data Is Used to Improve Model Performance." OpenAI Help Center, 2024. https://help.openai.com/en/articles/5722486-how-your-data-is-used-to-improve-model-performance ↩

  2. Vancouver School Board. "Copilot 13+ in Secondary Schools." Vancouver School Board, 2026. https://www.vsb.bc.ca/copilot-13-in-secondary-schools ↩

entry.title: Part 3 - ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity: A Plain-Language Comparison for Parents
entry.summary: A plain-language guide to the five AI tools your children are most likely to encounter — what each one does, who made it, and which one to try first.
entry.content:
Part 3 of 12 in the Try It Yourself series — practical AI guidance for parents at blog.parentsguidetoai.ca

Once you decide to try AI yourself, the next question is: which one?

If you search for "AI tools" right now, you'll find hundreds of options. Most of them you can ignore, at least until you learn more about AI tools and which ones you might want to use, or if you need to learn more about one your children or students ask about. The parent's guide at parentsguidetoai.ca names five tools specifically — and those five are the ones your children are most likely to encounter. So let's focus there.

This post gives you a plain-language rundown of each one. What it is, what it's good for, who made it, whether there's a free version, and where the age limits sit. By the end, you'll know which one to try first and why.

The five tools

ChatGPT — OpenAI

ChatGPT is the tool that kicked off the current wave of public interest in AI. OpenAI released it in late 2022,1 and within a few months it had more users than almost any product in history.2

It works as a conversation. You type something. It responds. You can ask follow-up questions, push back on its answers, ask it to rewrite something in a different tone, or have it help you think through a problem. In addition to text chats, ChatGPT is a multi-modal tool, which means it can produce more than just text, it can also produce images, documents, and other types of content.

Good for: General questions, writing help, brainstorming, explaining concepts in plain language, creative tasks.

Free tier: Yes. The free version is genuinely useful. A paid version (ChatGPT Plus) exists and adds faster access and some additional features.

Who made it: OpenAI, an American AI research company.

Age minimum: 13 for a personal account (18 without parental permission in some regions). Children under 13 are not permitted.3

One thing to know: ChatGPT is the most widely used AI tool in the world, which means it's probably the one your child has already tried or heard about.

Microsoft Copilot — Microsoft

Copilot is Microsoft's AI. It's built on the same underlying technology as ChatGPT (OpenAI's models), but it's integrated into Microsoft's products — including Word, Excel, Outlook, and Teams.

For parents of VSB students, Copilot is the most relevant tool on this list. The Vancouver School Board has introduced Copilot 13+, a managed school version of Copilot for eligible secondary students.4 We'll cover what makes that different from the home version in Post 7.

The public version of Copilot works in your browser with no account required to start. That makes it the easiest of the five tools to try without committing to anything.

Good for: General questions, research, summarizing documents, and — for VSB families — understanding what your child is using at school.

Free tier: Yes. No account needed to start.

Who made it: Microsoft, in partnership with OpenAI.

Age minimum: 13 for a Microsoft account. The school version (Copilot 13+) is managed by the school district.5

One thing to know: If you have a Microsoft account (which you might through work, or through an Office subscription), you may already have access to Copilot features you haven't noticed yet.

Google Gemini — Google

Gemini is Google's AI. If you use Gmail, Google Docs, or Google Search, you may have already encountered Gemini features appearing as suggestions or summaries — often without realizing they're AI.

The standalone Gemini tool is a conversation interface similar to ChatGPT and Copilot. Because it's made by Google, it has strong integration with Google's other services and tends to be well-connected to recent information from the web.

Good for: Research, summarizing information, working alongside Google products you already use, translation.

Free tier: Yes.

Who made it: Google (Alphabet).

Age minimum: 13 for a personal Google account (18 for users in the EU and UK).6 Accounts through Google Workspace for Education (used by some schools) have different settings.

One thing to know: For Canadian parents: Gemini's age minimum for a personal account is 13, the same as most other tools on this list.

Claude — Anthropic

Claude is made by Anthropic, a company founded by former members of OpenAI. It tends to be particularly strong at reading and understanding long documents, writing in a consistent voice, and handling nuanced or careful topics.

Claude is less well-known than ChatGPT or Copilot, but it's widely used among writers, researchers, and people who want a more careful, thorough response style.

Good for: Reading and summarizing long documents, careful writing, nuanced explanations, tasks that benefit from a more thoughtful response.

Free tier: Yes. The free version has some usage limits.

Who made it: Anthropic, an American AI safety company.

Age minimum: 18 for a personal account.7

One thing to know: Claude tends to be more careful about expressing opinions or taking strong positions, which some people find more appropriate for research and learning contexts.

Perplexity — Perplexity AI

Perplexity is a bit different from the others. It's designed specifically as a research and search tool. Instead of just generating a response, Perplexity shows you the sources it drew on — inline, as part of the answer. This makes it significantly easier to verify what it's telling you.

For parents who want to check whether AI-generated information is accurate — which Post 5 is all about — Perplexity is the most useful tool on this list.

Good for: Research, fact-checking, understanding a topic from multiple sources, looking up information you want to be able to verify.

Free tier: Yes.

Who made it: Perplexity AI, a San Francisco-based startup.

Age minimum: 13.8

One thing to know: Perplexity is the best choice when you need to verify something. Unlike the other tools, it shows its sources by default rather than presenting information without attribution.

How they compare at a glance

Tool Free? Min. age Best for Made by
ChatGPT Yes 13 General use, writing OpenAI
Copilot Yes 13 VSB families, research Microsoft / OpenAI
Gemini Yes 13 Google users, research Google
Claude Yes (limited) 18 Long documents, careful writing Anthropic
Perplexity Yes 13 Verification, research with sources Perplexity AI

Which one should you start with?

If your child is a VSB secondary student: start with Copilot. It's the tool most directly connected to what your child may be using at school, and the home version is easy to access without an account.

If your main goal is to get better at fact-checking: start with Perplexity. It's the most transparent about where its information comes from, which makes it a natural fit for the verification skills we cover in Post 5.

If you just want the most widely-used tool: start with ChatGPT. It's what most people mean when they say "AI" in casual conversation, and it's a useful baseline.

Pick one. You don't need all of them. They're more similar than they are different, and using one well is more valuable than having accounts you never open.

The short version:

  • Five tools are named in the parent's guide: ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
  • They all do similar things but have different strengths.
  • For VSB families: Copilot is the most relevant. For verification: Perplexity is the best choice.
  • Check the age minimums before letting your child set up a home account — Gemini requires 18.
  • Pick one and try it. You can always explore others later.

Now that you know which tool to use and why, the next post covers something important before you go further: what you should never type into any of these tools.

Try it today: Sign up for a free account on whichever tool you chose. Just creating the account and sending one test message counts. To stretch yourself, try out two or three, and ask each one the exact same questions and observe the difference in how they respond.

Next up: Post 4 — Your Privacy Checklist: What Never to Share With AI

Notes

  1. OpenAI. "Introducing ChatGPT." OpenAI, November 30, 2022. https://openai.com/index/chatgpt/ ↩

  2. Hu, Krystal. "ChatGPT Sets Record for Fastest-Growing User Base." Reuters, February 2, 2023. https://www.reuters.com/technology/chatgpt-sets-record-fastest-growing-user-base-analyst-note-2023-02-01/ ↩

  3. OpenAI. "Is ChatGPT Safe for All Ages?" OpenAI Help Center, 2024. https://help.openai.com/en/articles/8313401-is-chatgpt-safe-for-all-ages ↩

  4. Vancouver School Board. "Copilot 13+ in Secondary Schools." Vancouver School Board, 2026. https://www.vsb.bc.ca/copilot-13-in-secondary-schools ↩

  5. Microsoft Support. "Microsoft Copilot Age Limits and Parental Controls." Microsoft, 2024. https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/topic/microsoft-copilot-age-limits-and-parental-controls-f79b47a6-288a-4513-8c01-afe4d16db900 ↩

  6. Google Support. "What You Need to Sign In to Gemini Apps." Google, 2024. https://support.google.com/gemini/answer/13278668 ↩

  7. Anthropic. "Consumer Terms of Service." Anthropic, 2024. https://anthropic.com/legal/terms ↩

  8. Perplexity AI. "Terms of Service." Perplexity AI, 2024. https://www.perplexity.ai/hub/legal/terms-of-service ↩

entry.title: Want to Build an AI Guide for Parents in Your Province or Territory? Here's Where to Start.
entry.summary: A Parent's Guide to AI was written for BC. Most of it applies across Canada — but school policies, privacy laws, and local resources vary by province. Here's a practical roadmap for building a version specific to where you live.
entry.content:
Since publishing A Parent's Guide to Artificial Intelligence, I've heard from parents and educators in other provinces who want something similar — but specific to their jurisdiction. That makes sense. Education is a provincial responsibility under the Canadian constitution.1 The school board policies, the privacy laws, the specific tools being deployed, and the local crisis resources all differ depending on where you live.

The national resources — MediaSmarts, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner, Cybertip.ca, Kids Help Phone — apply everywhere. The rest needs local research.

This post is a practical roadmap for anyone who wants to build a province- or territory-specific version of this guide. The original guide is published under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 licence, which means you're welcome to adapt it as long as you credit the source, don't use it commercially, and share your adaptation under the same licence. The source files are on GitHub. Not only that, if you or your community want to build a guide for your province or territory, I will happily adapt https://parentsguidetoai.ca to support regional guides and recommendations!

Why a province-specific guide matters

The core content about AI — what it is, how it works, why it sounds confident when it's wrong — is the same everywhere. But a parent reading a guide needs answers to questions that are specific to their situation:

  • Which AI tools has our school board approved or deployed?
  • What does our province's privacy law say about how student data can be used?
  • If something goes wrong, who do we call?
  • What does our province's curriculum say about digital literacy and AI?

A guide that says "check with your school board" doesn't answer those questions. A guide built on local research does.

Resources to locate for each province or territory

Start with official sources. These are the most important things to track down before you write a word of the guide itself.

1. Ministry or Department of Education guidance on AI

Every province and territory has a Ministry (or Department) of Education. Most have published, or are in the process of publishing, guidance on AI use in schools. The quality and specificity of this guidance varies significantly — some provinces have detailed frameworks, others have a brief statement acknowledging that AI exists.

Look for: - Any formal policy or administrative directive on AI use in K-12 settings - Guidance documents for teachers and administrators - Statements on acceptable use of AI for student work - Any provincial digital citizenship or technology strategy

File a freedom of information request if policies aren't publicly posted. Timelines and fees vary by province — in BC, FIPPA allows 30 business days to respond, with no fee for personal requests.

2. Provincial or territorial privacy legislation

Canada's provinces and territories have their own privacy laws that may overlap with or supplement federal legislation (PIPEDA).2 These vary significantly:

  • Quebec has the strongest student data protections in Canada under Law 25 (Act respecting the protection of personal information in the private sector), which requires explicit consent and data minimization
  • Alberta and BC have their own private-sector privacy statutes (PIPA)
  • Ontario, Manitoba, Nova Scotia, and others rely primarily on federal PIPEDA for private-sector data, but have public-sector privacy laws that cover schools
  • Territories are largely governed by federal law

Your guide needs to tell parents what protections actually exist in their jurisdiction — not just what they should hope for.

3. School board policies — especially the largest boards

Provincial guidance sets the floor. Individual school boards set their own policies on top of it, and those can vary widely. Start with the two or three largest boards in the province, since they tend to lead on policy development and often have the most detailed documentation.

Look for: - Acceptable use policies that mention AI specifically - Any board-level decisions on specific AI tool deployments (like the VSB's Copilot 13+ rollout) - Data governance or vendor agreements for AI tools used in schools - Parent communication that has gone out about AI

Board meeting minutes are usually public. If a board approved an AI tool deployment, it will be in the minutes.

4. The provincial privacy commissioner

Every province and territory has an Information and Privacy Commissioner (or equivalent) whose jurisdiction includes how public bodies — including schools — handle personal information. Most commissioners' offices have published resources on children's privacy and emerging technologies.

The commissioner's guidance is often more accessible than the legislation itself, and more directly useful for parents trying to understand their rights.

5. Provincial teacher federation positions

Teacher federations in Canada are often ahead of government on emerging education policy questions, including AI. They represent frontline educators who are navigating these issues in classrooms every day, and their position statements and professional guidance documents are usually well-researched.

Every province has a teacher federation. Find their published statements on AI in education, and look for any professional learning resources they've produced for members.

6. Provincial digital citizenship and curriculum frameworks

Most provinces have integrated digital literacy or digital citizenship expectations into their curriculum frameworks, though the depth and specificity varies. Some provinces (BC, Ontario, and PEI notably) have more developed frameworks than others.

These frameworks are worth including in a provincial guide because they define what children are supposed to learn about navigating digital environments — including AI — and give parents a reference point for what to expect from schools.

7. Any legislation specifically addressing AI, children's data, or online harms

This is a moving target. Several provinces are actively developing or have recently passed legislation that intersects with AI and children's rights. Nova Scotia, Ontario, and Quebec have all moved on related issues in the last few years. Check for:

  • Legislation on online harms or cyberbullying
  • Amendments to education acts that address student data
  • Legislation on non-consensual intimate images (most provinces now have this)

8. Indigenous education frameworks and commitments

Each province and territory has different commitments under the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's Calls to Action, and different relationships with First Nations, Métis, and Inuit communities and governments. AI has specific implications for Indigenous students and families — including questions about whose knowledge and perspectives are reflected in training data, and about data sovereignty.

A responsible provincial guide needs to address this, and to do so it needs to understand the specific context of the province. Look for:

  • Provincial Indigenous education frameworks
  • School board Indigenous education strategies
  • Statements from First Nations education authorities in the province
  • Any Métis Nation guidance on data and technology

Organizations to connect with for feedback

Building a guide without community input is how you end up with a guide that answers the wrong questions. These are the types of organizations whose feedback will make the guide more accurate and more useful.

Parent and family organizations

  • Provincial parent advisory federations — most provinces have a provincial body that represents school-level parent advisory councils. In BC that's BCCPAC; in Ontario it's the Ontario Federation of Home and School Associations (OFHSA); in Alberta it's the Alberta School Councils' Association (ASCA).3 These organizations know what questions parents are actually asking.
  • School-level parent advisory councils — talking to a handful of PACs from urban, suburban, and rural contexts will surface very different concerns and information gaps.
  • Newcomer and settlement organizations — families who are new to Canada may face additional barriers around language, familiarity with the Canadian school system, and trust in institutions. Their perspective is essential for making the guide genuinely accessible.
  • Disability advocacy organizations — AI tools have significant accessibility implications. Organizations representing parents of children with learning disabilities, autism spectrum conditions, or physical disabilities will have specific questions and insights.

Educators and school staff

  • Teacher federations — you'll already have read their published guidance; ask if you can speak with their professional development leads or their policy staff
  • School librarians and teacher-librarians — often the most practically informed people in a school on questions of information literacy, research, and AI tools
  • School counsellors and social workers — they deal with the harm side of this directly; their input on what goes wrong and what parents need to know is valuable
  • Indigenous education workers and cultural support workers — essential for the Indigenous education section of any provincial guide

School trustees and district staff

Trustees are elected officials accountable to the community. District technology directors and privacy officers deal with the operational questions — vendor agreements, student data, acceptable use policy — directly. Both are worth talking to.

Provincial and territorial government offices

  • Information and Privacy Commissioner staff — they will often speak informally to people developing public interest resources about how the legislation applies in practical terms
  • Ministry of Education curriculum staff — useful for understanding how AI fits (or doesn't fit) into the provincial curriculum framework
  • Children's Commissioner or Advocate — most provinces and territories have an independent officer responsible for children's rights; their perspective on AI's implications for children is worth seeking

National organizations with local reach

  • MediaSmarts — Canada's centre for digital and media literacy has regional contacts and may be able to connect you with people doing similar work in your province
  • Canadian Centre for Child Protection (which runs Cybertip.ca and NeedHelpNow.ca) — can speak to the harm patterns they're seeing and what resources are most needed in your region
  • Local chapters of national organizations — community legal clinics, public libraries, and children's mental health organizations often have provincial or regional branches that can review the guide for local accuracy

Post-secondary education

Faculties of Education at provincial universities often have researchers working on exactly these questions. Academic review adds credibility and catches errors; in return, the guide can help their research reach a public audience.

Questions your guide needs to answer

Use these as a checklist when you're evaluating whether you have enough local research to actually write:

  • Which specific AI tools are being used in schools in this province, and under what policies?
  • What does provincial privacy law require before a school can share student data with an AI vendor?
  • Is parental consent required? At what age can a student consent independently?
  • What are the largest school boards doing, and where can parents find their policies?
  • What does the provincial teacher federation recommend that teachers do and not do with AI?
  • What provincial curriculum expectations address digital literacy and AI?
  • What are the provincial-specific crisis resources (for deepfakes, NCII, sextortion, cyberbullying)?
  • Who is the provincial privacy commissioner, and what resources have they published?
  • What Indigenous data sovereignty considerations apply in this province?
  • Are there French-language resources available for Francophone families?

If you can answer all of those questions with sources, you have enough material for a useful guide.

Practical notes on building and maintaining a provincial guide

Start with the national baseline. The BC guide covers the national content — AI fundamentals, national organizations, federal legislation — that applies anywhere in Canada. Don't re-research what's already documented. Fork the existing content (it's CC-licensed), replace the BC-specific sections with your province's equivalents, and add the local resources.

Date everything. AI policy is moving fast. Every policy reference, every resource link, every statistic should have a date attached. Readers need to know how current the information is.

Publish it openly. A guide that lives behind a paywall or a login doesn't reach the parents who need it most. Publish it under a Creative Commons licence so others can share, translate, and adapt it.

Plan for maintenance. A guide published once and never updated will be misleading within a year. Either commit to reviewing it at the start of each school year, or build a community of contributors who can flag changes. GitHub is a reasonable infrastructure for this — version history is public, anyone can open an issue to flag something outdated, and pull requests allow community contributions.

Get a plain-language review. Academic reviewers will catch errors. A plain-language review by someone without a background in education or technology will catch everything that's technically accurate but still not useful to a non-expert parent. Both matter.

If you're building a guide for your province or territory and have questions, open an issue on the GitHub repository or reach out through the blog. I'm glad to share what worked and what didn't in developing the BC version.

Read the original guide: parentsguidetoai.ca

Fork or adapt the source files: github.com/ygjb/parentsguidetoai.ca — published under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0

Notes

  1. Constitution Act, 1867, s. 93. https://laws-lois.justice.gc.ca/eng/const/page-6.html ↩

  2. Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada. "The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)." Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada, 2024. https://www.priv.gc.ca/en/privacy-topics/privacy-laws-in-canada/the-personal-information-protection-and-electronic-documents-act-pipeda/ — Note: Bill C-27 (Consumer Privacy Protection Act), which would have replaced PIPEDA, died on the order paper when Parliament was prorogued in January 2025. PIPEDA remains in force as of June 2026. ↩

  3. BC Confederation of Parent Advisory Councils (BCCPAC): https://bccpac.bc.ca — Ontario Federation of Home and School Associations (OFHSA): https://www.ofhsa.on.ca — Alberta School Councils' Association (ASCA): https://www.albertaschoolcouncils.ca ↩

entry.title: Part 2 - What AI Actually Is (And Why That Matters for Parents)
entry.summary: AI works like a very sophisticated autocomplete — it predicts words, it doesn't think. Understanding this changes how you respond when your child brings home AI-assisted work.
entry.content:
Part 2 of 12 in the Try It Yourself series — practical AI guidance for parents at blog.parentsguidetoai.ca

Your child brings home an essay. It's well-written. The sentences flow. The ideas are organized. You're pretty sure they didn't write it — or at least, not all of it. But you don't know how to tell. And you're not sure what to say.

To have that conversation well, you need to understand what AI actually does. Not in a technical way. But in the same way you might understand what Google does for people who use it — well enough to explain it to someone else.

This post covers that. It's shorter than you might expect, because the core idea is actually pretty simple.

It's a very sophisticated autocomplete

The parent's guide at parentsguidetoai.ca describes AI this way, and I think it's the best explanation I've seen for a non-technical audience:

"One way to think about AI is like the autocomplete feature on your phone. It looks at many examples and predicts what is likely to come next."

That's the core of it. When you type a question into ChatGPT or Copilot, the system doesn't "think" about your question the way a person would. It predicts — at enormous speed and scale — what words are most likely to follow, based on patterns it has seen in billions of pieces of text.

It's autocomplete that has read most of the internet.

This is why AI can write a convincing paragraph on almost any topic. It has seen a huge number of examples of how paragraphs about that topic are supposed to sound. It's incredibly good at matching patterns. But pattern-matching is not the same as understanding.

One of the most common ways I use AI on a daily basis is to assist with security analysis of the applications and services I support at work. While the tools are flexible and powerful, they are also confidently wrong quite frequently. In security, we call these false positives, which are bad, because the AI has claimed that a vulnerability exists where there isn't one, but even worse are the false negatives - an AI might be used for analysis and decide there are no security flaws, which could lead to a false sense of security. In order to avoid that, the way that I use AI tools is to augment my workflow, and support the analysis I perform, not to replace the work I do.

Why AI sounds confident even when it's wrong

This is the part that matters most for parents.

Because AI is predicting what words should come next, not checking facts, it can be wrong with complete confidence. It doesn't know it's wrong. It has no way to know. It's producing the most statistically likely response, not the most accurate one.

This has a name in the AI world: hallucination.1 It's when an AI system generates information that is incorrect, made up, or unsupported — but states it as though it were fact.

Hallucinations happen most often with:

  • Citations and sources — AI will invent plausible-sounding academic papers that don't exist
  • Statistics and numbers — the specific figure sounds right but may be fabricated
  • Names and dates — details about real people, events, or places can be quietly wrong
  • Local or recent information — things that happened recently, or information specific to a region like BC, are more likely to be wrong2

The danger is that hallucinated information looks identical to correct information. There's no red flag. The AI doesn't say "I'm not sure about this." It just says it.

We'll spend a whole post on how to catch these mistakes (Post 5). For now, the important thing is to know they exist and happen regularly.

What this means for your child's work

Once you understand that AI is a sophisticated pattern-matcher — not a thinker — a few things become clearer.

First, AI-generated writing tends to sound like a lot of other AI-generated writing. Because it's drawing on the most common patterns it has seen, the output tends toward the average. It's competent. It's rarely surprising. Experienced teachers are getting better at recognizing this.

Second, AI can be wrong on factual details — including details in a homework assignment. If your child uses AI to research something and doesn't verify the information, they may submit something that sounds authoritative but contains errors.

Third, there's a real distinction between using AI to understand something and using AI to produce something. The parent's guide puts it well in Section 8:

"A useful question for parents to ask is: 'Could you explain this work without using the AI?'"

That's not a trick question. It's a genuine check. If your child can explain the ideas in their own words, they've learned something. If they can't, the AI may have done the learning for them.

A quick note on what AI is not

The parent's guide is clear on this: "AI systems do not think or understand the world the way humans do."

This is worth saying plainly, because a lot of media coverage treats AI as though it's either a genius or a monster. It's neither. It's a very powerful tool that is very good at certain things and genuinely unreliable at others.

It doesn't have opinions. It doesn't have feelings. It doesn't have goals. When it seems to, that's the pattern-matching at work — it has seen a lot of human text where people express opinions and feelings, so it produces text that sounds like that.

Understanding this doesn't make AI less useful. It makes it more useful, because you know what it's actually good for.

The short version:

  • AI works like a very sophisticated autocomplete — it predicts likely words, it doesn't "think."
  • This is why it can sound confident and still be completely wrong.
  • These errors (called hallucinations) are most common in citations, statistics, dates, and local or recent information.
  • The key question to ask your child: "Could you explain this work without using the AI?"
  • AI is a powerful tool. It's not a genius, and it's not a threat — it's a pattern-matcher with real limitations.

Now that you have a working mental model of what AI is, the next post gets more practical: we'll walk through the five main tools mentioned in the parent's guide, compare them in plain language, and help you decide which one is worth trying first.

Try it today: Open any AI tool from Post 1 and ask it about a topic you already know well — something from your job, your hobby, or your city. Read the response and notice one thing it gets slightly wrong or oversimplifies. That's the pattern-matcher at work.

Next up: Post 3 — ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity: A Plain-Language Comparison for Parents

Notes

  1. Google Cloud. "What Are AI Hallucinations?" Google Cloud, 2024. https://cloud.google.com/discover/what-are-ai-hallucinations ↩

  2. DataCamp. "AI Hallucinations: A Guide with Examples." DataCamp Blog, 2024. https://www.datacamp.com/blog/ai-hallucination ↩

entry.title: Part 1 - Before You Talk to Your Kids About AI, Try It Yourself
entry.summary: The best way to guide your children through the AI era is to try these tools yourself first. Here's how to get started in five minutes.
entry.content:
Part 1 of 12 in the Try It Yourself series — practical AI guidance for parents at blog.parentsguidetoai.ca

My very first experience with parenting and AI came from a day my daughter spent at Ethọ́s Lab. My daughter is very artistic, and routinely makes some absolutely fantastic art (I have a bias, but also she is actually really talented :D) That day she brought home an extremely complex piece of art that was obviously created digitally, but it was completely unlike anything she had ever made before. I already had a great deal of experience with Midjourney and Stable Diffusion, and almost immediately recognized that it was AI generated. She was insistent that she had created it herself and came up with the idea for it, but it took a while to get her to explain the tools she had used. She also got very frustrated because she had worked with the tool she used to get the result over multiple iterations. Back in early 2023, I was also a bit of an AI skeptic (and still remain adamantly attached to the idea that AI art isn't art because art requires human expression and intention). This conversation was the first time I had to think about how the the technology I was learning about AI and working with at work was going to impact parenting, and I was poorly prepared.

That's a moment most parents hit at some point, if you haven't already. Your kid mentions an AI tool you've never heard of. They ask you to explain what AI is. They give you a look. And you realize you're the least informed person in the conversation.

That moment is fixable, and it's really only takes a few minutes and some free tools.

I'm not suggesting you become an expert. You don't need to be. But there's a real difference between reading about AI and actually using it. This whole series is built on that idea: the best way to guide your children through the AI era is to try these tools yourself first.

I want to remind you, this blog, and my guide are a volunteer effort. I work with these technologies in my job every day, but I don't have a stake in promoting or using any tools. Like my guide, I don't use tracking technology on this blog, and I don't include referral links or get any rewards or sponsorships for the tools the I suggest here.

Why trying it yourself changes everything

Think about learning to drive. You can read every manual ever written about a car. But until you've actually sat behind the wheel, you don't really know what it feels like to merge onto a highway or parallel park on a hill. Advice from someone who's driven is just different from advice from someone who's read about it.

AI is the same way.

When you've used an AI tool, even just once, for five minutes, you will see and start to understand things that are hard to explain from the outside. You know that it sometimes sounds incredibly confident while being completely wrong. You know that the quality of what you get back depends heavily on how you ask the question. You know that it can feel strangely easy to trust.

Those are exactly the things your kids need to understand. And you're in a much better position to have that conversation if you've already been there.
